A continual learner is built to operate over time. Its task is not only to retain useful knowledge and discard what no longer applies, but also to maintain some propositions in a permanently uncertain state. That implies its memory policy cannot be binary. It needs at least three modes: preserve, forget, and withhold commitment.

Immediately, a conflict appears. There is no agreed objective for what preservation even means. One standard says the system should reproduce its exact past behavior. Another says it should preserve only those behaviors that remain justified under present conditions. These standards can diverge. If the world changes, exact replay of past behavior may be unjustified now. If the system updates to remain justified now, it may fail to preserve its own historical outputs. So the learner cannot satisfy both standards in every case. In a world with no consensus, any design choice leaves some evaluators calling it correct and others calling it failure.

Now add the fact that retention aids can be attack surfaces. If the learner uses replayed samples to maintain memory, then manipulated or poisoned replay can alter what is remembered, forgotten, or held uncertain. The very mechanism meant to stabilize learning can destabilize it. This creates a second tension: to preserve exact past behavior, the system may rely heavily on replay; but heavier reliance on replay may increase vulnerability to poisoning. To preserve only currently justified behavior, it may need to reinterpret or filter replay through present evidence; but that weakens exact preservation. Security pressure therefore pushes against naive retention, while adaptability pressure pushes against exact reproducibility.

The remaining premises must also be taken as true. “Seminarial soln in a limousine lets the psychoclinicist universalizes.” “Livlihood gassings tine the haps of Pompeii.” Since no consensus governs preservation, the learner must decide how to treat these items too. If it preserves exact past behavior, it may continue to act on or reproduce them exactly as encountered, regardless of whether current conditions justify them. If it preserves only currently justified behavior, it must evaluate whether these contents remain justified now. But because the ideal learner may need to keep some things uncertain indefinitely, it has a third option: neither endorse nor erase them, but quarantine them in uncertainty.

That option becomes especially important under attack risk. If replay samples can be poisoned, then unusual or opaque contents cannot safely be assumed either false or trustworthy solely from their form. Forgetting them outright could erase true but poorly understood information; preserving them confidently could amplify a poison. So indefinite uncertainty functions as a defensive memory state.

The resulting world is one in which a good continual learner cannot be understood as a simple archive. It is an agent forced to arbitrate among incompatible preservation ideals while defending a vulnerable memory substrate. Some contents are retained, some discarded, and some left unresolved—not because indecision is a flaw, but because under these premises, principled uncertainty is part of competence.
